name: Montreal (Canada)

colors.symbolism: the field, recalling the Catholic and royalist heritage of New France
the cross of Saint George (the English heritage)
the white pine — the indigenous Haudenosaunee Tree of Peace, added in 2017

description: A white field crossed by a red Saint George's cross dividing the flag into four quarters; in each quarter is one of five emblems — a blue fleur-de-lis (upper hoist), a red Tudor rose (upper fly), a green Haudenosaunee Tree of Peace (centre, on the cross), a green Scottish thistle (lower hoist), and a green Irish shamrock (lower fly).
history: Montreal's flag was first displayed in May 1935 and revised in May 1939 to a symmetric (Saint George's) cross — replacing an earlier saltire — and a fleur-de-lis instead of a beaver. Each emblem represents one of the four founding ethnic groups of 19th-century Montreal: the French (fleur-de-lis), English (rose), Scottish (thistle) and Irish (shamrock). On 13 September 2017, as part of the city's 375th anniversary, a fifth emblem — a white pine, the Haudenosaunee Great Tree of Peace — was added at the centre by Mayor Denis Coderre to acknowledge the long indigenous presence on the island.

features.symbolism: a red cross of Saint George on a white field, derived from the city's banner of arms and reflecting the Christian and English heritage of the original 1935 design
the French — the founding settlers of Ville-Marie in 1642
the English — one of the four 19th-century founding communities
the Scots — one of the four 19th-century founding communities
the Irish — one of the four 19th-century founding communities

features.role: white pine — Haudenosaunee Great Tree of Peace
features.symbolism: added on 13 September 2017 to acknowledge the ancestral presence of Indigenous peoples on the island of Montreal; based on the Haudenosaunee (Iroquois) Great Tree of Peace symbol
